title: Jawbone mesenchymal stromal cells attenuate acute inflammation via hematopoietic niche reinforcement
research_question: How do jawbone mesenchymal stromal cells (MSCs) interact with hematopoietic stem cells (HSCs) to regulate immune responses and attenuate acute inflammation?
methods_summary: Optimized enzymatic digestion to obtain single-cell suspension from mouse jawbone
Single-cell RNA sequencing (scRNA-seq) to analyze cell interactions
Tissue immunofluorescence to assess spatial distribution of MSCs and HSCs
In vitro osteogenic and adipogenic differentiation assays
Transwell co-culture system to study MSC effects on HSC differentiation
Flow cytometry to quantify immune cell types (B cells, T cells, myeloid cells)
RT-qPCR and immunofluorescence to detect transcription factors (e.g., PAX5)
LPS-induced acute infection mouse model to evaluate therapeutic efficacy
ELISA to measure serum cytokines (IFN-γ, IL-6)
dataset: Mouse jawbone tissue (C57BL/6, Col1-CreERT2; tdTomato+ lineage tracing mice)
key_findings: Jawbone MSCs and HSCs are spatially proximate and exhibit strong ligand-receptor interactions (e.g., CXCL12-CXCR4).
Jawbone MSCs possess multilineage differentiation potential (osteogenic and adipogenic).
Co-culture with jawbone MSCs promotes HSC differentiation into B cells, T cells, and myeloid cells, with a pronounced effect on B cells.
Jawbone MSCs upregulate key B cell transcription factors, particularly PAX5.
In an LPS-induced acute infection model, jawbone MSC treatment improves survival and reduces systemic inflammation (lower IFN-γ and IL-6).
Jawbone MSCs secrete CXCL12, which is implicated in hematopoietic regulation.
extracted_insights: Jawbone MSCs reinforce the hematopoietic niche by secreting CXCL12 and activating PAX5, promoting HSC differentiation into B cells, thereby attenuating acute inflammation and improving survival in an LPS-induced infection model.
